subtopic_detail: Discriminating schizoaffective disorder from bipolar I with mood-congruent psychosis
stem: A 32-year-old man has had three discrete depressive episodes over 4 years, each with mood-congruent paranoid delusions and auditory hallucinations. Between episodes, his depressive symptoms have remitted but the paranoid delusions and auditory hallucinations have persisted continuously for at least 3 weeks at a time, in the absence of an acute mood episode. He has no history of manic or hypomanic episodes. He uses no substances. There is no first-degree family history of bipolar disorder.

data_table.Examination: Today: persecutory delusions present; mood euthymic; speech normal; no manic features
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative; TSH 1.6 mIU/L; CT brain normal; PANSS positive 28

lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, depressive type, given the persistence of psychotic symptoms for 2 weeks or longer in the absence of an acute major depressive episode in this patient
options.rationale: DSM-5 schizoaffective disorder requires major mood episodes during a substantial portion of the illness with at least 2 weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of an acute mood episode. (Source: DSM-5; RANZCP schizoaffective disorder clinical resource)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features, given his recurrent psychotic mood symptoms with full remission of psychotic symptoms between acute mood episodes in his presentation
options.rationale: He has had no manic or hypomanic episodes and psychosis persists between mood episodes; bipolar I does not fit. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder, given the episodic nature of his psychotic symptoms over 4 years of intermittent illness, with mood symptoms considered incidental in this patient
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder is by definition under 1 month and does not recur over years. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features, given the depressive episodes and concurrent psychotic features, with psychosis considered episode-bound in this patient's longitudinal course of illness
options.rationale: MDD with psychotic features requires psychosis to occur only during depressive episodes; persistent psychosis between episodes excludes this. (Source: DSM-5)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder, given the under-6-month duration of any individual psychotic episode in this patient with intermittent mood symptoms and recurring discrete intervals of psychosis
options.rationale: Schizophreniform applies for psychotic episodes lasting 1 to 6 months in total; the longitudinal course here exceeds this. (Source: DSM-5)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder requires DSM-5 criteria of an uninterrupted period of illness with a major mood episode (manic or depressive) concurrent with schizophrenia A criteria, plus at least 2 weeks of delusions or hallucinations in the absence of a major mood episode. Mood episodes are present for the majority of the active and residual phases. Differentiation from bipolar I with psychotic features rests on persistence of psychosis outside of mood episodes; from MDD with psychotic features on the same point; from schizophrenia on the prominence and duration of mood symptoms.
key_points: Schizoaffective disorder requires psychosis for 2 weeks or more in the absence of an acute mood episode
Mood episodes must be present for the majority of the illness's active and residual phases
Mood-congruent psychosis confined to mood episodes points to MDD or bipolar I with psychotic features
explanation.pearls: Schizoaffective disorder is identified longitudinally: at least 2 weeks of psychosis without an active mood episode, with mood symptoms across the majority of the total illness duration distinguishing it from primary psychotic disorders.

subtopic_detail: Acute behavioural disturbance in the ED requiring rapid tranquillisation
stem: A 36-year-old man brought in by police is highly agitated, shouting and pacing in the ED short-stay unit. He has paranoid delusions and a history of treatment-resistant schizophrenia, with intermittent methamphetamine use. He is intermittently approaching staff aggressively but has not yet assaulted anyone. He is refusing oral medication and verbal de-escalation has failed. There is no documented allergy, no current QT prolongation on ECG, and no concurrent CNS depressant ingestion suspected.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/86, HR 108, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.0 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, dilated pupils 5 mm, ECG sinus tachycardia QTc 412 ms, blood alcohol 0
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen pending; previous ECG 3 weeks ago QTc 408 ms; weight 80 kg

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Single oral diazepam 2 mg only without any antipsychotic, with no further escalation pathway and no re-evaluation interval planned for this patient with acute behavioural disturbance
options.rationale: A low oral benzodiazepine dose alone is sub-therapeutic for severe acute behavioural disturbance and lacks a planned escalation pathway. (Source: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance clinical practice guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG

options.text: De-escalation first, then offer oral olanzapine 10 mg or oral lorazepam 1 to 2 mg; if refused or unsafe, intramuscular olanzapine 10 mg or droperidol 10 mg with cardiac and respiratory monitoring
options.rationale: AU stepped pathway prioritises de-escalation and oral options before parenteral therapy; IM olanzapine or droperidol is the preferred parenteral option with monitoring of airway and ECG. (Source: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance clinical practice guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG

options.text: Single intramuscular haloperidol 20 mg as a one-off dose without ECG, without monitoring and without re-evaluation, regardless of comorbid stimulant intoxication or QTc status
options.rationale: High-dose haloperidol risks dystonia, QT prolongation and torsades, especially with stimulant intoxication; not recommended as a first-line single dose. (Source: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG)
source_refs: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG

options.text: Rapid IV midazolam infusion at 5 mg/hour without monitoring, escalation plan or airway support, in a general ward bed with no immediate access to flumazenil or other airway adjuncts
options.rationale: IV midazolam infusion outside a high-acuity bed risks respiratory arrest; IM benzodiazepines or olanzapine are safer first-line parenteral choices. (Source: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG)
source_refs: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG

options.text: Physical restraint indefinitely without medication, with no clear monitoring plan, no medical review interval and no de-escalation attempt offered to the patient before restraint is applied
options.rationale: Mechanical restraint without pharmacotherapy and ongoing medical review is unsafe and ethically untenable; it is a last-resort short-term measure with chemical and behavioural alternatives. (Source: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG)
source_refs: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG

explanation.summary: Acute behavioural disturbance is managed by a stepped pathway: assess for medical or substance causes, attempt verbal de-escalation and environmental measures, offer oral medication (olanzapine, lorazepam or risperidone), then escalate to intramuscular olanzapine or droperidol if refused or unsafe. Monitoring includes airway, conscious level, SpO2, BP, HR, ECG (for QT), temperature and post-restraint review. Mechanical restraint is a last resort and time-limited. Specific AU pathways (NSW Health, Victorian DHHS, Queensland Health) operationalise this approach with consistent agents and review timing.
key_points: De-escalation and oral medication precede parenteral options where safe
IM olanzapine or droperidol is the preferred parenteral option with monitoring
Mechanical restraint is a last resort, time-limited and requires ongoing medical review
explanation.pearls: Intramuscular olanzapine 10 mg or droperidol 10 mg with airway, SpO2 and ECG monitoring is the AU-preferred parenteral rapid tranquillisation when oral medication is refused or unsafe.

sources.label: NSW Health acute behavioural disturbance CPG
RANZCP guideline on management of acute behavioural disturbance

subtopic_detail: Abnormal uterine bleeding in a peri-menopausal woman with obesity
stem: A 47-year-old woman presents with 6 months of increasingly heavy and irregular menstrual bleeding. Her BMI is 34 kg/m2 and she has type 2 diabetes. She has had two children and uses no hormonal contraception. She has no postcoital bleeding, no urinary symptoms and is up to date with cervical screening. She has not noticed any weight loss or systemic symptoms. There is no first-degree relative with endometrial or colorectal malignancy.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, bulky uterus on bimanual; cervix appears macroscopically normal
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L, MCV 78 fL, ferritin 14 microgram/L, beta-hCG negative, TSH 1.4 mIU/L; cervical screening test 6 months ago: HPV not detected

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Initiate a levonorgestrel intrauterine system empirically without any imaging or histological evaluation of the endometrium and review the patient in 6 months for ongoing review
options.rationale: Endometrial assessment is mandatory in women 45 or older with abnormal uterine bleeding and obesity given hyperplasia risk; empirical hormonal therapy without histology is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG abnormal uterine bleeding statement)

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound to measure endometrial thickness only, without any plan for endometrial biopsy regardless of findings or risk factors in this patient's presentation today
options.rationale: TVUS alone is insufficient in pre-menopausal women: the negative predictive value of a thin endometrium is uncertain; histology is required given the risk profile. (Source: RANZCOG abnormal uterine bleeding statement)

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound to assess endometrial thickness and structural lesions plus endometrial biopsy by pipelle, given her age, obesity and diabetes, with management determined by histology
options.rationale: Women 45 or older with abnormal uterine bleeding, plus those younger with persistent symptoms or risk factors (obesity, anovulation, diabetes, tamoxifen, Lynch), require imaging and endometrial sampling. (Source: RANZCOG abnormal uterine bleeding statement)

options.text: Proceed to dilatation and curettage under general anaesthesia in the next 4 weeks without any prior pipelle sampling or transvaginal ultrasound being attempted at this initial outpatient assessment
options.rationale: Pipelle sampling in outpatients is first-line; D&C is reserved for inadequate pipelle or persistent bleeding after pipelle. (Source: RANZCOG abnormal uterine bleeding statement)

options.text: Commence the combined oral contraceptive pill empirically as the first investigation and management step without imaging or histological evaluation of the endometrium in this peri-menopausal patient
options.rationale: Empirical hormonal therapy without histology risks masking endometrial pathology in a high-risk profile. (Source: RANZCOG abnormal uterine bleeding statement)

explanation.summary: Women 45 or older with abnormal uterine bleeding, and women younger than 45 with persistent symptoms or risk factors (obesity, anovulation, diabetes, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome, unopposed oestrogen), require imaging (TVUS) and endometrial sampling (pipelle biopsy in outpatients). Hysteroscopy and dilatation and curettage are reserved for inadequate or non-diagnostic outpatient sampling. Management depends on histology: hyperplasia without atypia responds to progestogen (LNG-IUS), atypical hyperplasia or carcinoma is managed surgically.
key_points: AUB in women 45 or older mandates TVUS plus endometrial biopsy
Younger women with obesity, diabetes or anovulation also need sampling
Hysteroscopy and D&C are reserved for inadequate or non-diagnostic pipelle
explanation.pearls: A pipelle biopsy in the outpatient setting is the AU first-line endometrial sampling method; sensitivity for endometrial cancer in a peri-menopausal woman is around 90 to 95 percent with adequate sampling.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia: endometrial cancer guideline

subtopic_detail: Preconception counselling and medication review in type 2 diabetes
stem: A 32-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 5 years presents for preconception counselling. Her current medications include metformin, an ACE inhibitor for diabetic nephropathy with mild albuminuria, and a statin. Her HbA1c is 8.4 percent. She is on the combined oral contraceptive for cycle regulation. She wants to start trying for pregnancy in 6 to 12 months. She has had no diabetic retinopathy on her last screen, and has had no microvascular complications outside mild albuminuria.

data_table.Examination: Normal cardiovascular examination, no peripheral neuropathy on monofilament
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 8.4%, eGFR 86, urine albumin-creatinine ratio 8 mg/mmol, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, B12 220 pmol/L, fundoscopy at 12 months unremarkable

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate preconception plan?

options.text: Continue all current medications including the ACE inhibitor and statin into pregnancy with no specific glycaemic target and no folate prescribing initiated at this visit
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors and statins are contraindicated in pregnancy; HbA1c targets pre-pregnancy aim for less than 6.5 percent where safe; folate at 5 mg daily is needed. (Source: ADIPS consensus guidelines for pregestational diabetes; RANZCOG preconception care)

options.text: Continue all current medications into pregnancy unchanged on the basis that her glycaemic control has been adequate over the last 12 months at the current regimen for this patient
options.rationale: HbA1c 8.4 percent is above the preconception target and ACE inhibitors and statins are contraindicated in pregnancy. (Source: ADIPS guidelines for pregestational diabetes)

options.text: Cease all diabetes medications before any future conception, with no glycaemic targets set, no folate prescribing initiated and no further follow-up arranged before the next clinic visit
options.rationale: Diabetes therapy intensifies to optimise control pre-pregnancy, not stop entirely; metformin and insulin are pregnancy-compatible. (Source: ADIPS guidelines for pregestational diabetes)

options.text: Aim for HbA1c under 6.5 percent before conception with insulin (metformin may continue), cease ACE inhibitor and statin before conception, start folic acid 5 mg daily, schedule retinal and renal review
options.rationale: Australian preconception care for pregestational diabetes targets HbA1c under 6.5 percent (without significant hypoglycaemia), substitutes pregnancy-compatible agents, ceases teratogens, and adds 5 mg folate. (Source: ADIPS guidelines for pregestational diabetes; RANZCOG preconception care)

options.text: Counsel her to avoid pregnancy until HbA1c is under 5 percent, with no other medication adjustments, no folate prescribing initiated, and no further microvascular complication screening at this visit
options.rationale: An HbA1c under 5 percent is below the population mean and unachievable safely; ADIPS target is under 6.5 percent without significant hypoglycaemia. (Source: ADIPS guidelines for pregestational diabetes)

explanation.summary: Preconception care in pregestational type 2 diabetes targets HbA1c under 6.5 percent (without significant hypoglycaemia), switches to pregnancy-compatible agents (insulin, metformin), ceases teratogens (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, statins, SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists), and starts folic acid 5 mg daily for at least 1 month before conception until 12 weeks gestation. Microvascular complication screening (retinal, renal, neuropathy) is undertaken pre-pregnancy and during pregnancy. Contraception is maintained until glycaemic and medication targets are met.
key_points: Preconception HbA1c target under 6.5 percent without significant hypoglycaemia
Cease ACE inhibitors, ARBs, statins, SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists pre-conception
Folic acid 5 mg daily for at least 1 month before conception in pregestational diabetes
explanation.pearls: ADIPS recommends folic acid 5 mg daily (not 0.5 mg) for at least 1 month before conception in pregestational diabetes given elevated neural tube defect risk.

subtopic_detail: Suspected non-accidental injury in a non-mobile infant
stem: A 5-month-old girl is brought to ED by her mother after being noticed to have multiple bruises in different stages of healing. She is not yet rolling or crawling. Chest radiograph performed for an unrelated cough shows a healing posterior rib fracture. There is no documented family history of bleeding disorders or osteogenesis imperfecta. The history of injury offered is vague and changes between caregivers. Other siblings are unwell with no clear medical cause documented.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 132, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: Multiple bruises over trunk and limbs in different stages of healing, frenal tear noted on upper lip examination, anterior fontanelle full
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, INR 1.0, fibrinogen 2.6 g/L, ALT 28 U/L; calcium and ALP within reference range; CXR shows healing posterior rib fracture

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Discharge home with verbal safety advice for the parents, with a GP review in 4 weeks for general well-child check and no further specialist or child protection involvement initiated at this time
options.rationale: The combination of bruising in a non-mobile infant, frenal tear, sentinel posterior rib fracture and inconsistent history mandates inpatient evaluation, safeguarding referral and mandatory reporting. (Source: RCH CPG: suspected non-accidental injury; AIFS mandatory reporting)

options.text: Arrange GP follow-up in 6 weeks for review of the bruises and the healing rib fracture, with no specific paediatric or child protection involvement requested before that GP review
options.rationale: Delayed outpatient review fails the safeguarding threshold; sentinel injuries demand same-day inpatient evaluation. (Source: RCH CPG: suspected non-accidental injury)

options.text: Treat the rib fracture conservatively with simple analgesia and discharge home with no further investigation, no consultation with child protection services, and no follow-up at any later date
options.rationale: Posterior rib fractures in non-mobile infants carry a very high specificity for inflicted injury and require comprehensive workup with safeguarding. (Source: RCH CPG: suspected non-accidental injury)

options.text: Telephone parents to clarify the history of the injury and arrange a home visit by a community nurse in 24 hours, with no immediate inpatient workup, safeguarding referral or mandatory report submitted
options.rationale: Reliance on caregiver-reclarification in a sentinel-injury picture risks ongoing harm; mandatory reporting and inpatient workup are required. (Source: AIFS mandatory reporting)

options.text: Admit for full skeletal survey, fundoscopy, head imaging and coagulation profile to exclude mimics, plus urgent paediatric and child protection team consultations and a mandatory report to statutory services
options.rationale: Suspected non-accidental injury in a non-mobile infant requires inpatient comprehensive evaluation (skeletal survey, fundoscopy, neuroimaging, coagulation and bone profile to exclude mimics), child protection consultation and statutory mandatory reporting. (Source: RCH CPG: suspected non-accidental injury; AIFS mandatory reporting)

explanation.summary: Sentinel injuries (bruises in a non-mobile infant, frenal tears, posterior rib fractures) carry high specificity for inflicted injury and trigger inpatient comprehensive evaluation. Workup excludes mimics (coagulopathy, osteogenesis imperfecta, metabolic bone disease) and documents the pattern (skeletal survey under 2 years, neuroimaging, fundoscopy for retinal haemorrhage when intracranial findings or under 2 years). All Australian states mandate reporting suspected child abuse by medical practitioners. Child protection and social work involvement, multidisciplinary case management and safe disposition planning follow.
key_points: Bruising in a non-mobile infant is highly suspicious for inflicted injury until proven otherwise
Posterior rib fractures in infants have high specificity for inflicted injury
Mandatory reporting by medical practitioners applies in all Australian states and territories
explanation.pearls: TEN-4 FACES rule: bruises in non-mobile infants under 4 months, or torso, ear or neck bruises in infants under 4 years, are sentinel injuries warranting safeguarding workup until proven otherwise.
